title: Ear infections in rabbits: recognition and treatment
subtitle: Distinguishing ear mites from otitis media before both progress.
excerpt: A rabbit shaking its head and scratching at one ear has either ear mites or an ear canal infection (otitis externa), or both. A rabbit with a head tilt has an infection of the middle ear (otitis media). The two conditions look different, need different treatments, and carry very different prognoses. Distinguishing them early determines the outcome.
sourceNotes: Merck Veterinary Manual, ear diseases in rabbits (online edition). RWAF ear disease information, rwaf.org.uk. DEFRA Code of Practice for the Welfare of Rabbits (2019).

glossaryTerms.definition: Infection of the middle ear in rabbits, usually caused by Pasteurella multocida or E. coli spreading from the outer canal through the eardrum, or via the Eustachian tube from the nasal cavity. Presents as head tilt, circling, and balance loss. Prognosis depends on how early treatment begins.
Common term for a heavy Psoroptes cuniculi (ear mite) infestation producing thick, dry, grey-brown crusts inside the ear pinnae. Highly contagious between rabbits.

content.text: Ear issues in rabbits range from a minor mite infestation, treated with a single spot-on, to an inner ear infection needing months of antibiotics. The signs overlap. Check both ears under good light before deciding.

Ear mites build up into dry, grey-brown crusts inside the ear flap. The rabbit shakes its head and scratches. The crusts spread deeper if left untreated. Treatment is a spot-on product from the vet (ivermectin or selamectin applied to the back of the neck). Do not pick out the
crusts before treatment, doing so damages the sensitive ear canal lining and causes bleeding.

presents as head tilt, from a slight angle to a severe roll with balance loss. The rabbit may circle toward the affected ear, show rapid eye movement (nystagmus), and be unable to eat or drink. See a vet the same day. Start antibiotics within 24 hours for the best outcome. Head tilt from inner ear infection can improve over several months; full resolution is not always possible.

A rabbit with a severe head tilt cannot navigate normally. Pad the enclosure with foam or towels to prevent head injuries from rolling. Place food and water within easy reach from a lying position. Many rabbits adapt to a permanent head tilt over months and live a good life; the decision to euthanise is based on quality of life rather than the tilt itself.

Head tilt in rabbits is also linked to E. cuniculi (Encephalitozoon cuniculi), a tiny parasite that affects the brain and balance system. A vet may prescribe fenbendazole as well as antibiotics when E. cuniculi cannot be ruled out. Both conditions can occur together.
